questions.stem: Two point masses lie on the $x$-axis: $2\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 0$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 5\ \text{m}$. Where is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The centre of mass is the mass-weighted average position: $x_{cm} = (m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2)/(m_1 + m_2) = (2 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 5)/(2 + 3) = 15/5 = 3\ \text{m}$. It lies closer to the heavier mass. The trap is $2.5\ \text{m}$, the plain midpoint, which would be correct only if the two masses were equal.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $1\ \text{kg}$ mass sits at the origin and a $3\ \text{kg}$ mass sits at $x = 4\ \text{m}$. How far from the $1\ \text{kg}$ mass is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: With the $1\ \text{kg}$ mass at the origin, $x_{cm} = (1 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 4)/4 = 12/4 = 3\ \text{m}$. The centre of mass sits three quarters of the way toward the heavier $3\ \text{kg}$ mass. The trap is $1\ \text{m}$, which places it nearer the light mass, inverting the weighting.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Three masses are placed in the plane: $2\ \text{kg}$ at $(0, 0)$, $2\ \text{kg}$ at $(4, 0)$, and $4\ \text{kg}$ at $(2, 3)$, all in metres. Where is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The total mass is $8\ \text{kg}$. The $x$-coordinate is $(2 \cdot 0 + 2 \cdot 4 + 4 \cdot 2)/8 = 16/8 = 2\ \text{m}$, and the $y$-coordinate is $(2 \cdot 0 + 2 \cdot 0 + 4 \cdot 3)/8 = 12/8 = 1.5\ \text{m}$. So the centre of mass is at $(2, 1.5)\ \text{m}$. The trap is $(2, 1)\ \text{m}$, which averages the three $y$-values $0, 0, 3$ without weighting by mass, giving $3/3 = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Three equal masses sit at the corners of a triangle with vertices $(0, 0)$, $(6, 0)$, and $(0, 6)$, in metres. Where is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: For equal masses the centre of mass is the plain average of the position vectors, the centroid: $x_{cm} = (0 + 6 + 0)/3 = 2\ \text{m}$ and $y_{cm} = (0 + 0 + 6)/3 = 2\ \text{m}$, so $(2, 2)\ \text{m}$. The trap is $(3, 3)\ \text{m}$, the midpoint of the hypotenuse, which ignores the mass at the origin.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ mass moves at $3\ \text{m/s}$ along the $x$-axis while a $4\ \text{kg}$ mass is at rest. What is the velocity of the centre of mass of the system?
questions.explanation: The centre-of-mass velocity is the total momentum divided by the total mass: $v_{cm} = (2 \cdot 3 + 4 \cdot 0)/(2 + 4) = 6/6 = 1\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $1.5\ \text{m/s}$, which divides the moving mass's momentum by $4$ rather than by the total mass $6$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two particles of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ move along a line with velocities $4\ \text{m/s}$ and $-1\ \text{m/s}$ respectively. What is the total momentum of the system?
questions.explanation: Total momentum adds the individual momenta with their signs: $p = 2(4) + 3(-1) = 8 - 3 = 5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m/s}$. The trap is $11\ \text{kg}\,\text{m/s}$, which adds $8 + 3$ and ignores that the second particle moves the other way.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A system of particles experiences no net external force. Which statement about its centre of mass is correct?
options: The centre of mass must be at rest.
The centre of mass accelerates in the direction of the internal forces.
Nothing can be said about the centre of mass without knowing the internal forces.
The centre of mass moves with constant velocity.
questions.explanation: The centre of mass obeys $M\mathbf{a}_{cm} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}$. With zero net external force, $\mathbf{a}_{cm} = 0$, so the centre of mass moves at constant velocity (possibly zero). Internal forces come in third-law pairs and cancel, so they never accelerate the centre of mass. The trap is the claim that it must be at rest; constant velocity includes the case of steady motion, not only rest.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A shell flying through the air explodes into fragments. Ignoring air resistance, how does the centre of mass of the fragments move immediately after the explosion?
options: It continues along the original parabolic trajectory the shell would have followed.
It moves in the direction of the largest fragment.
It moves straight up, following the explosion.
It stops, since the explosion cancels the motion.
questions.explanation: The explosion is driven entirely by internal forces, which cannot change the total momentum or the motion of the centre of mass. The only external force is gravity, so the centre of mass keeps moving along the same parabola the intact shell would have traced. The trap is that it stops; internal forces redistribute momentum among fragments but leave the centre-of-mass motion untouched.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $60\ \text{kg}$ person stands at one end of a $120\ \text{kg}$ boat that floats on still water with negligible resistance. The person walks $4\ \text{m}$ along the boat relative to the boat. How far does the boat move relative to the water?
questions.explanation: With no external horizontal force, the centre of mass stays fixed, so the momenta balance: $m_p d_p = m_b d_b$, where the displacements add to the $4\ \text{m}$ relative motion, $d_p + d_b = 4$. From $60 d_p = 120 d_b$ we get $d_p = 2 d_b$, so $2d_b + d_b = 4$ and $d_b = 4/3 \approx 1.33\ \text{m}$. The trap is $2\ \text{m}$, which splits the $4\ \text{m}$ equally and ignores that the heavier boat moves less.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A rocket ejects exhaust gas at a speed of $3000\ \text{m/s}$ relative to the rocket, burning fuel at a rate of $5\ \text{kg/s}$. What thrust does the engine produce?
questions.explanation: The thrust is the exhaust speed times the rate at which mass is ejected: $F = v_{ex}\,(dm/dt) = 3000 \times 5 = 15{,}000\ \text{N}$. The trap is $600\ \text{N}$, which divides the exhaust speed by the burn rate instead of multiplying, $3000/5$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A rocket in deep space has exhaust speed $2000\ \text{m/s}$ relative to itself. By what factor must its mass decrease for the rocket to gain a speed of $2000\ \text{m/s}$, starting from rest? Use the rocket equation $\Delta v = v_{ex}\ln(m_0/m_f)$.
questions.explanation: Setting $\Delta v = v_{ex}$ gives $2000 = 2000\ln(m_0/m_f)$, so $\ln(m_0/m_f) = 1$ and $m_0/m_f = e \approx 2.72$. The rocket must shed enough fuel that its initial mass is $e$ times its final mass. The trap is $2$, which assumes the mass ratio equals the speed ratio linearly and ignores the logarithm in the rocket equation.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two particles of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ interact only with each other. What is the reduced mass of the two-body system?
questions.explanation: The reduced mass is $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1 + m_2) = (2 \times 3)/(2 + 3) = 6/5 = 1.2\ \text{kg}$. It is always smaller than either mass. The trap is $5\ \text{kg}$, the total mass $m_1 + m_2$, which is what appears in the centre-of-mass motion, not in the relative motion.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $4\ \text{kg}$ mass at $x = 1\ \text{m}$ and a $1\ \text{kg}$ mass at $x = 6\ \text{m}$ lie on the $x$-axis. Where is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The centre of mass is $x_{cm} = (4 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 6)/(4 + 1) = (4 + 6)/5 = 10/5 = 2\ \text{m}$, close to the heavier mass at $x = 1\ \text{m}$. The trap is $3.5\ \text{m}$, the midpoint of the two positions, which would be the answer only for equal masses.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform rod of length $L$ has a point mass equal to the rod's own mass attached at one end. Measuring from the loaded end, where is the centre of mass of the combined object?
questions.explanation: Let the rod and the point mass each have mass $m$. The rod's own centre of mass is at its midpoint, $L/2$ from the loaded end, while the point mass is at $0$. The combined centre of mass is $(m \cdot 0 + m \cdot L/2)/(2m) = L/4$ from the loaded end. The trap is $L/2$, which ignores the point mass and reports only the rod's centre.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: The total external force on a system of particles equals which quantity?
options: The total mass times the acceleration of the centre of mass.
The mass of the heaviest particle times its acceleration.
The sum of all internal and external forces.
questions.explanation: Summing Newton's second law over all particles, the internal forces cancel in third-law pairs, leaving $\mathbf{F}_{ext} = M\mathbf{a}_{cm}$, where $M$ is the total mass. The system as a whole responds to external forces as though it were a single particle of mass $M$ at the centre of mass. The trap is the sum of internal and external forces; internal forces always cancel and contribute nothing to the net.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two masses, $1\ \text{kg}$ and $4\ \text{kg}$, are connected by a compressed spring and released from rest on a frictionless surface. When released, the $1\ \text{kg}$ mass moves off at $8\ \text{m/s}$. What is the speed of the $4\ \text{kg}$ mass?
questions.explanation: The system starts at rest, so total momentum stays zero: $m_1 v_1 = m_2 v_2$, giving $v_2 = (1 \times 8)/4 = 2\ \text{m/s}$ in the opposite direction. The spring is internal, so it cannot move the centre of mass. The trap is $32\ \text{m/s}$, which multiplies by the mass ratio the wrong way, $8 \times 4$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $3\ \text{kg}$ mass moves at $2\ \text{m/s}$ east and a $2\ \text{kg}$ mass moves at $3\ \text{m/s}$ north. What is the speed of the centre of mass of the system?
questions.explanation: The total momentum has an east component $3 \times 2 = 6\ \text{kg}\,\text{m/s}$ and a north component $2 \times 3 = 6\ \text{kg}\,\text{m/s}$. Dividing by the total mass $5\ \text{kg}$, the centre-of-mass velocity components are $1.2\ \text{m/s}$ each, so the speed is $\sqrt{1.2^2 + 1.2^2} = \sqrt{2.88} \approx 1.70\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $2.5\ \text{m/s}$, which averages the two given speeds without accounting for their perpendicular directions or the mass weighting.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A conveyor belt drops sand onto a moving cart at a steady rate. As more sand lands, what happens to the cart-plus-sand system if no horizontal force is applied to keep it moving?
options: Its momentum decreases, because the sand slows it down.
Its speed decreases, because the same momentum is shared by an increasing mass.
Its speed stays the same, because momentum is conserved.
Its speed increases, because the sand adds energy.
questions.explanation: The sand lands with no horizontal velocity, and with no horizontal external force the horizontal momentum of the system is conserved. As the mass grows, the same momentum $p = Mv$ requires the speed $v$ to drop. The trap is that the speed stays the same: momentum being conserved is exactly why the speed must fall as the mass rises.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $5\ \text{kg}$ mass and a $5\ \text{kg}$ mass start at rest at positions $x = 0$ and $x = 10\ \text{m}$. Internal forces then pull them together. Where do they meet?
options: They cannot meet without an external force.
questions.explanation: With equal masses starting at rest and only internal forces acting, the centre of mass stays fixed at $x_{cm} = (5 \cdot 0 + 5 \cdot 10)/10 = 5\ \text{m}$. The two masses must meet at the centre of mass, so they meet at $x = 5\ \text{m}$. The trap is the claim that they cannot meet; internal forces are perfectly able to bring them together at the stationary centre of mass.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A rocket of total mass $1000\ \text{kg}$ ejects gas. At one instant the thrust is $20{,}000\ \text{N}$ and the rocket is far from any planet (no gravity). What is its acceleration at that instant?
questions.explanation: With no gravity, the thrust is the only external-style force on the rocket body, so $a = F/m = 20{,}000/1000 = 20\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $2\ \text{m/s}^2$, which divides by $10{,}000\ \text{kg}$ or otherwise slips a factor of ten in the mass.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ mass at the origin and a $6\ \text{kg}$ mass at $x = 8\ \text{m}$ form a system. The system's centre of mass is then required to be at the origin by adding a third mass at $x = -4\ \text{m}$. What third mass is needed?
questions.explanation: For the centre of mass at the origin, the mass moments must sum to zero: $2(0) + 6(8) + m_3(-4) = 0$, so $48 = 4m_3$ and $m_3 = 12\ \text{kg}$. The new mass on the negative side must balance the moment of the $6\ \text{kg}$ mass on the positive side. The trap is $6\ \text{kg}$, which matches the existing mass rather than its moment and ignores the different lever arms ($8\ \text{m}$ versus $4\ \text{m}$).
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: The total kinetic energy of a system of particles can be written as the kinetic energy of the centre of mass plus what other term?
options: The potential energy of the system.
The kinetic energy of motion relative to the centre of mass.
The work done by internal forces.
Zero, since all the energy is in the centre of mass.
questions.explanation: Konig's theorem splits the total kinetic energy into two parts: $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}Mv_{cm}^2$, the bulk motion of the whole system, plus the kinetic energy of the particles measured in the centre-of-mass frame. An explosion at rest, for instance, has zero centre-of-mass kinetic energy but plenty of internal kinetic energy. The trap is the claim that all the energy is in the centre of mass; internal relative motion carries energy too.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A man of mass $80\ \text{kg}$ and a boy of mass $40\ \text{kg}$ stand $9\ \text{m}$ apart on frictionless ice and pull on a rope between them. How far does the man move before they meet?
questions.explanation: They meet at the fixed centre of mass. The man and boy cover distances inversely proportional to their masses: $d_{man}/d_{boy} = m_{boy}/m_{man} = 40/80 = 1/2$, with $d_{man} + d_{boy} = 9\ \text{m}$. So $d_{man} = 3\ \text{m}$ and $d_{boy} = 6\ \text{m}$. The trap is $4.5\ \text{m}$, the halfway point, which would be right only for equal masses.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ particle is at $(1, 2)\ \text{m}$ and a $3\ \text{kg}$ particle is at $(6, -3)\ \text{m}$. What is the $y$-coordinate of the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The $y$-coordinate of the centre of mass is $y_{cm} = (2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot (-3))/(2 + 3) = (4 - 9)/5 = -5/5 = -1\ \text{m}$. The trap is $-0.5\ \text{m}$, the unweighted average of $2$ and $-3$, which forgets to weight by the masses.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A rocket burns fuel and its speed increases. From the point of view of momentum, why does the rocket accelerate forward?
options: Burning fuel creates momentum out of nothing.
The exhaust pushes against the surrounding air.
The rocket gains forward momentum equal and opposite to the backward momentum carried away by the exhaust.
The rocket loses mass, and lighter objects automatically speed up.
questions.explanation: The rocket and its exhaust form an isolated system, so total momentum is conserved. Ejecting gas backward gives that gas backward momentum, and the rocket must gain an equal forward momentum to keep the total unchanged. This is why rockets work in the vacuum of space. The trap is that the exhaust pushes against the air; rockets accelerate even where there is no air, precisely because the mechanism is momentum conservation, not pushing on a medium.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two point masses lie on the $x$-axis: $2\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 0$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 5\ \text{m}$. Find the centre of mass and state which mass it lies closer to.
questions.solution: The centre of mass is $x_{cm} = (2 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 5)/(2 + 3) = 15/5 = $ {{1}} metres. It lies closer to the {{2}} mass.
questions.explanation: The mass-weighted average is $15/5 = 3\ \text{m}$, which sits between the masses but nearer the $3\ \text{kg}$ one. The centre of mass always leans toward the heavier mass.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Three masses are placed in the plane: $2\ \text{kg}$ at $(0, 0)$, $2\ \text{kg}$ at $(4, 0)$, and $4\ \text{kg}$ at $(2, 3)$, in metres. Find the coordinates of the centre of mass.
questions.solution: With total mass $8\ \text{kg}$, the $x$-coordinate is $(0 + 8 + 8)/8 = $ {{1}} and the $y$-coordinate is $(0 + 0 + 12)/8 = $ {{2}} metres.
questions.explanation: The $x$-coordinate is $16/8 = 2\ \text{m}$ and the $y$-coordinate is $12/8 = 1.5\ \text{m}$, so the centre of mass is at $(2, 1.5)\ \text{m}$. Each coordinate is weighted by mass separately.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ mass moves at $3\ \text{m/s}$ east while a $4\ \text{kg}$ mass is at rest. Find the total momentum of the system and the velocity of the centre of mass.
questions.solution: The total momentum is $2 \times 3 + 4 \times 0 = $ {{1}} kilogram metres per second, directed east. Dividing by the total mass of $6\ \text{kg}$, the centre-of-mass velocity is {{2}} metres per second.
questions.explanation: The momentum is $6\ \text{kg}\,\text{m/s}$, and $v_{cm} = 6/6 = 1\ \text{m/s}$. The centre of mass carries the whole system's momentum at this single velocity.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A rocket ejects exhaust at $3000\ \text{m/s}$ relative to itself, burning $5\ \text{kg}$ of fuel each second. Its total mass at one instant is $2000\ \text{kg}$ and it is far from gravity. Find the thrust and the instantaneous acceleration.
questions.solution: The thrust is $v_{ex}\,(dm/dt) = 3000 \times 5 = $ {{1}} newtons. The acceleration is this thrust divided by the rocket's mass, $a = $ {{2}} metres per second squared.
questions.explanation: The thrust is $15{,}000\ \text{N}$, and $a = 15{,}000/2000 = 7.5\ \text{m/s}^2$. Thrust depends on exhaust speed and burn rate; the resulting acceleration also depends on the current mass.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two particles of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and $3\ \text{kg}$ interact only with each other. Find the total mass and the reduced mass of the two-body system.
questions.solution: The total mass is $m_1 + m_2 = $ {{1}} kilograms. The reduced mass is $m_1 m_2/(m_1 + m_2) = 6/5 = $ {{2}} kilograms, which is always {{3}} than either individual mass.
questions.explanation: The total mass is $5\ \text{kg}$ and the reduced mass is $6/5 = 1.2\ \text{kg}$. Since $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1+m_2)$, it is always less than the smaller of the two masses.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $60\ \text{kg}$ person walks $4\ \text{m}$ along a $120\ \text{kg}$ boat (relative to the boat) that floats on still water with negligible resistance. Find how far the boat moves relative to the water.
questions.solution: With the centre of mass fixed, $60 d_p = 120 d_b$ so $d_p = 2 d_b$, and the displacements satisfy $d_p + d_b = 4\ \text{m}$. Solving, $d_b = 4/3 \approx $ {{1}} metres, while the person moves {{2}} metres relative to the water.
questions.explanation: From $3d_b = 4$, the boat moves $d_b = 4/3 \approx 1.33\ \text{m}$ and the person moves $d_p = 8/3 \approx 2.67\ \text{m}$ the other way. The heavier boat moves less, keeping the centre of mass stationary.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics
